name: Nephilim
description: The Nephilim are a mysterious and controversial race mentioned in the Hebrew Bible and related apocryphal texts. The Book of Genesis describes them as being on the earth in the days before the flood, and also afterward, suggesting multiple appearances. They are typically interpreted as the offspring of the 'sons of God' (bene Elohim) and 'daughters of men.' Their immense size and strength are often emphasized, leading to their association with giants. The exact nature of the 'sons of God' is debated, with interpretations ranging from fallen angels to descendants of Seth intermarrying with the line of Cain. The presence of the Nephilim is often linked to the increasing wickedness of humankind, ultimately leading to God's decision to cleanse the earth with a great flood. Their existence and role continue to be a subject of theological discussion and interpretation.
subtitle: Giant offspring of divine beings and humans.
appearance: Typically depicted as giants, towering over ordinary humans. Their physical appearance is not explicitly described in detail in canonical texts, leading to varied artistic representations. Some depictions portray them as possessing a somewhat grotesque or unnatural appearance, reflecting their hybrid nature.
habitat: Primarily associated with the antediluvian world, before the Great Flood. Later accounts suggest their presence in Canaan.
behavior: Generally portrayed as violent and corrupt, contributing to the wickedness that prompted the Great Flood. They are often characterized as oppressive and tyrannical, using their strength to dominate and exploit others.

keyMyths.title: Genesis 6:1-4
keyMyths.description: This passage in Genesis describes the 'sons of God' taking 'daughters of men' as wives, resulting in the birth of the Nephilim. It is the primary biblical source for their existence and connection to divine-human interbreeding. The passage is brief but has generated extensive interpretation and debate.

keyMyths.title: Book of Enoch
keyMyths.description: The Book of Enoch provides a more detailed account of the Nephilim's origins, attributing them to fallen angels called 'Watchers' who descended to Earth and mated with human women. This version emphasizes the corruption and destruction caused by the Nephilim and their angelic fathers, including teaching forbidden knowledge to humanity.

keyMyths.title: Numbers 13:33
keyMyths.description: During the Israelite reconnaissance of Canaan, some spies reported seeing giants, which they identified as descendants of Anak, whom they likened to Nephilim. This account instilled fear in the Israelites and contributed to their reluctance to enter the Promised Land, showcasing the lingering fear and reputation of the Nephilim.

sources.author: David Penchansky
sources.work: Twilight of the Gods: Polytheism in the Hebrew Bible
sources.description: Explores the context of the 'sons of God' in Genesis 6 and their relationship to ancient Near Eastern mythology.
sources.text: Explores the context of the 'sons of God' in Genesis 6 and their relationship to ancient Near Eastern mythology.

sources.author: John J. Collins
sources.work: The Apocalyptic Imagination: An Introduction to Jewish Apocalyptic Literature
sources.description: Provides a detailed analysis of the Book of Enoch and its significance in understanding the development of angelology and demonology, including the role of the Watchers and the Nephilim.
sources.text: Provides a detailed analysis of the Book of Enoch and its significance in understanding the development of angelology and demonology, including the role of the Watchers and the Nephilim.

sources.author: Carol A. Newsom
sources.description: Examines the Qumran texts, including fragments of Enoch, and their contributions to understanding the beliefs about angels, demons, and the origins of evil, relevant to the Nephilim narratives.
sources.text: Examines the Qumran texts, including fragments of Enoch, and their contributions to understanding the beliefs about angels, demons, and the origins of evil, relevant to the Nephilim narratives.
